title: Intake and Output and Fluid Balance Monitoring
subtitle: The nurse measures intake and output accurately, uses daily weights as the best fluid indicator, and recognizes fluid overload versus deficit — reporting urine output under 30 mL/hr.

patient.name: Intake & Output
patient.admitDate: The nurse measures and records intake and output and monitors fluid balance.

vitals.temp: PO, IV, tube feeds, ice chips

labs.v: Dry mucosa, poor turgor, low BP.

nursesNote: The nurse measures intake and output to assess fluid balance and hydration. Intake includes oral fluids, IV fluids, tube feedings, and foods that are liquid at room temperature (gelatin, popsicles, broth); ice chips are counted as about half their volume. Output includes urine, emesis, liquid stool, and drainage from tubes or wounds, all measured in a graduated container read at eye level. Daily weights — taken at the same time, on the same scale, in similar clothing — are the most sensitive indicator of fluid status, where about 1 kg of change equals roughly 1 liter of fluid. The nurse recognizes fluid overload (edema, crackles, weight gain, distended neck veins) versus deficit (dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, hypotension, concentrated urine) and reports a urine output under 30 mL/hr.
providerOrders: Record all intake and output in milliliters each shift
Count ice chips as about half their volume
Obtain daily weights at the same time on the same scale
Report urine output less than 30 mL/hr
Monitor for signs of fluid overload or deficit

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about intake and output?

opts.text: Intake and output tracks fluid balance and hydration status
Ice chips are recorded as part of intake
Liquid stool and emesis count as output
Solid food is recorded as fluid intake
Foods liquid at room temperature (gelatin, popsicles) count as intake
Wound and tube drainage are part of output
Daily weights are a sensitive indicator of fluid status
About 1 kg of weight gain equals roughly 1 liter of retained fluid
Output much greater than intake suggests fluid volume deficit
Urine output under 30 mL/hr is normal and needs no action
Edema, crackles, and weight gain suggest fluid overload
Daily weights should be done at the same time on the same scale

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order measuring urine output from a catheter bag

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and don gloves
Empty the drainage bag into a graduated container
Read the volume at eye level
Record the amount on the I&O record
Total the intake and output at the end of the shift
IV fluids
Urine
Ice chips
Emesis
Tube feeding
Liquid stool / diarrhea
Gelatin and popsicles
Wound or NG drainage

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Urine output is under 30 mL/hr — order the response
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recheck the measurement and ensure tubing is not kinked
Assess the patient's vitals, hydration, and recent intake
Notify the provider of persistent low output
Implement ordered interventions (e.g., fluids)
Continue to monitor hourly output
Counting ice chips as about half their volume
Discarding urine before it is measured
Weighing the patient at the same time daily on the same scale
Recording solid food as fluid intake
Reporting urine output under 30 mL/hr
Estimating output instead of measuring it
Reading the graduated container at eye level
Reporting a 2 kg overnight weight gain
